policy_statement: Implement plot conveyance competitions that specifically aim at carbon-neutrality, analyse experiences, and use lessons to develop plot conveyance terms and conditions (pilots during 2017–2021; experiences included by 2035).
mechanism_description: The City will implement plot conveyance competitions aimed at carbon-neutrality and use the lessons learned to develop plot conveyance terms and conditions that steer future development toward lower emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Land sale/lease conditions → low/zero-carbon building design + materials + energy systems → ↓ building lifecycle energy use and embodied carbon → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: The city alters how it disposes of/leases plots by running carbon-neutrality-focused competitions and embedding emissions-related set into plot conveyance terms (e.g., performance standards, design constraints, reporting).  
(2) Mediating process: Developers respond by selecting lower-carbon materials and construction methods, improving building envelope efficiency, installing efficient/electrified systems and/or on-site renewables, and optimizing operations—reducing fuel combustion and grid electricity demand and lowering embodied emissions.  
(3) Outcome: Lower operational and embodied GHG emissions from buildings and associated site development over the asset lifecycle.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from new development by mandating/incentivizing low-carbon building performance through land conveyance requirements.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance/contracting lever (plot conveyance terms) drives lower-carbon development choices, yielding direct GHG reductions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: The primary intended and largest effect is emissions reduction, not improving efficiency as an end in itself (Resource Efficiency) or reducing exposure to hazards (Adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: While some terms could include climate-resilience or resource-efficiency co-benefits (e.g., water efficiency, heat resilience), the mechanism as defined centers on “carbon-neutrality” and “lower emissions,” so mitigation dominates.  
4) Applies to all instances: Any policy using low-carbon land disposition terms/competitions has the primary effect of steering development toward lower-emission outcomes; the instrument may vary but the emissions-reduction pathway is consistent.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether reductions come mainly from operational energy, electrification, renewables, district energy, or embodied carbon measures requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If plot conveyance terms primarily mandate renewable generation/export without changing building demand, the pathway still classifies as Mitigation (clean energy substitution). If terms primarily address flood/heat resilience with minimal emissions intent, that would be a different mechanism and could shift toward Adaptation; not indicated here.
key_indicators: "implement plot conveyance competitions"; "aim particularly at carbon-neutrality"; "experiences ... analysed"; "used when developing the terms and conditions for plot conveyance"; "Pilots"
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy because it pilots and institutionalizes new land-disposition templates, but the operative lever is conditional tendering/contract terms tied to plot conveyance (closest to procurement-style requirements).
